questions.question: BFS and DFS differ in which data structure they use to track the frontier?
options: BFS uses a stack, DFS uses a queue
BFS uses a queue (FIFO), DFS uses a stack (LIFO, often via recursion)
questions.explanation: Queue (FIFO) gives level-by-level expansion — BFS. Stack (LIFO) gives depth-first expansion — DFS. The recursion stack in a recursive DFS plays exactly that role. Swap the data structure and you transform one algorithm into the other.

questions.question: What's the time complexity of BFS or DFS on a graph stored as an adjacency list?
questions.explanation: Each vertex is visited once (O(V)); each edge is examined at most twice in undirected graphs or once in directed (O(E)). Total O(V + E). With an adjacency matrix, each vertex's neighbor lookup is O(V) so total becomes O(V²).

questions.question: Why does BFS find shortest paths in an unweighted graph but Dijkstra is needed for weighted graphs?
options: BFS treats each edge as weight 1; vertices are dequeued in increasing distance order. In a weighted graph, a higher-weight edge could be the first one BFS would dequeue, but it shouldn't extend the path — a priority queue (Dijkstra) is needed to always pop the closest unvisited vertex.
questions.explanation: BFS's FIFO queue gives correct shortest paths only when each edge contributes the same cost. With weights, you need to extract the smallest-distance vertex next — that's a priority queue. Dijkstra ≈ BFS + priority queue. Phase 04 Lesson 14 builds it from scratch.

questions.question: Which traversal naturally yields topological order on a DAG?
options: DFS — reverse the order in which nodes finish (turn black). Equivalent to Kahn's BFS-style algorithm, but DFS-based is more concise.
questions.explanation: After DFS completes, reverse-finish-time gives a topological order. Why? A node finishes only after all its descendants finish; so reversing puts ancestors before descendants. Kahn's BFS algorithm achieves the same via in-degree counting (Lesson 06).

questions.question: A tree on n vertices has how many edges?
questions.explanation: Trees have exactly n - 1 edges. Proof by induction on n: base case is one vertex (0 edges). Each new leaf added contributes one edge. Equivalent characterizations: connected with n-1 edges, or acyclic with n-1 edges — either implies the other for an n-vertex graph.

questions.question: Why does 3-color DFS (white/gray/black) detect cycles in a directed graph?
options: A gray vertex is on the current DFS stack. Reaching a gray vertex from one of its descendants means following a back-edge — a cycle. Black vertices have finished and can be ignored.
questions.explanation: Three-color DFS classifies edges: tree edges (white target), forward / cross edges (black target — no cycle), back edges (gray target — cycle). A gray target means you're looking at an ancestor still on the DFS call stack — by definition a cycle through u and v.
